retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Spherocytes: Seen most commonly in hereditary spherocytosis due to mutations in genes that code for the red blood cell membrane proteins spectrin (alpha and beta), ankyrin, band 3 protein, protein 4.2, and other red blood cell membrane proteins that cause erythrocytes to change shape. [3] Disease entities manifesting spherocytes include hereditary spherocytosis (AKA Minkowski-Chauffard disease), ABO incompatibility, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (warm Antibody subtype), Cold agglutinin hemolysis, severer burns, myelodysplasia (MDS), hemolytic disease of the newborn, and myelophthisis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with SCA, peripheral blood smear shows elongated RBC with tapering ends that look like a sickle (also called drepanocytes). Additional findings are present in a few patients. Howell-Jolly bodies- Remnants of DNA are seen in the RBC and commonly seen in patients in whom the spleen has been removed. Therefore, patients with SCA have auto-splenectomy. Target cells (Leptocytes)- Most commonly seen in patients with Thalassemia. They are seen frequently in sickle-thalassemia syndromes and are sometimes noted in patients with SCA. Polychromatic cells - these are reticulocytes that signify marrow response to hemolysis. Nucleated red blood cells can sometimes be visible on the peripheral smear.

retrieved_docs.title: Thalassemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral blood smear: A blood smear (also called peripheral smear and manual differential) is next, to assess additional red cell properties. Thalassemia can present with the following findings on the peripheral blood smear: Microcytic cells (low MCV) Hypochromic cells Variation in size and shape (anisocytosis and poikilocytosis) Increased percentage of reticulocytes Target cells Heinz bodies

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Codocytes (target cells): Seen in thalassemia, liver disease (cholestatic type), hemoglobin C disorder, recently splenectomy, autosplenectomy in sickle cell disease, and iron deficiency disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Megaloblastic macrocytosis refers to enlarged erythrocytes that are comparable in size to leukocytes. Due to DNA and RNA synthesis disruptions, this condition is often associated with hypersegmented polymorphonuclear neutrophils. However, patients with liver failure may exhibit acanthocytes or spur cells, along with macro-ovalocytes, stemming from lipid dysfunction from hepatic insufficiency. Various histopathological findings may be observed in normocytic anemia resulting from hemolysis, including sickled cells in sickle cell anemia; spherocytes in conditions, such as hereditary spherocytosis and autoimmune hemolytic anemia; Heinz bodies; Howell-Jolly bodies; degmacytes; and schistocytes. The specific presentations depend on the intrinsic or extrinsic factors contributing to normocytic anemia. Diagnosing aplastic anemia necessitates a bone marrow aspirate, which typically appears dry due to the absence of cells or may contain multiple white adipocytes lacking erythrocytes, leukocytes, or progenitor cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Beta Thalassemia Major (Cooley Anemia) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Morphological changes of RBCs like microcytosis, hypochromia, poikilocytosis, anisocytosis, and nucleated RBCs can be seen on the peripheral blood smear. Additionally, target cells, teardrop cells, and basophilic stippling (course type) can also be visualized on the peripheral blood smear. The number of erythroblasts depends on the degree of anemia and after splenectomy. Moreover, either Hb electrophoresis or high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is warranted for definitive diagnosis. It analyzes Hb both quantitatively and qualitatively. Beta-thalassemia major demonstrates a complete absence of beta-globin chain (beta0/beta0), therefore the HbF is 92% to 95%, HbA2 is 5% to 8%, and HbA 0%.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: A bite cell has a semi-circular indentation as if someone took a bite from it. Echinocytes are erythrocytes with uniform projections all equidistant apart. Schistocyte is a term that refers to an RBC fragment that has been sheared. A sickle cell is an RBC with a crescentic shape. Spherocytes are erythrocytes that have no central pallor and are uniformly red throughout. Stomatocytes have a linear central clearing rather than a circular one. Target cells have a red center with a central clearing. Teardrop cells taper on one end. One last group of erythrocyte descriptors evaluated with light microscopy is inclusion bodies. These are cytoplasmic or nuclear findings that give clinical clues. For example, Heinz bodies are small round masses seen with supravital or Heinz stains that indicate hemolysis. Howell-Jolly bodies are solid, large, round masses found in the hemoglobin of the cell, are visualized with Wright-stain, and have many clinical significances. Pappenheimer bodies are multiple, small blue inclusion bodies throughout the entire RBC, indicating iron overload, hyposplenism, or myelodysplasia. Finally, basophilic stippling is blue/purple granules seen in thalassemia, lead poisoning, and other pathologies. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Erythroid lineage suppression: The presence of Kell antigens on erythroid precursors has been proven by suppression with anti-Kell antibodies on RBC production. These antibodies inhibit the colony-forming units and cause severe fetal anemia in the case of HDFN. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The routine use of highly accurate and reliable automated hematology analyzers in hematology and clinical pathology laboratories has reduced the use of peripheral blood smears as a diagnostic tool. RBC inclusions can be identified on a peripheral smear, providing concise and valuable clues to the diagnosis or indicating further investigative avenues to arrive at a final diagnosis. The structure, compositional characterization, and pathophysiological link of RBC inclusions may provide valuable clues to underlying disease or disorder. [2] Many erythrocyte inclusions, if present in significant amounts, can interfere with the data obtained from an automated hematology analyzer data. [3] In particular, Heinz bodies and Hemoglobin H can cause erroneous results in automated reticulocyte counts. [4] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- α-Thalassemia Carriers
retrieved_docs.content: α° trait is usually marked by slight anemia, slightly reduced MCV and MCH, RBC microcytosis, hypochromia, and anisopoikilocytosis. Hb Bart's may be detected in moderate amounts (3% to 8%) during the neonatal period. Some Heinz inclusion bodies may also be noted.

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drepanocytes or sickle cells: Seen is sickle cell disease due to hemoglobin S (HbS) by substituting valine in place of glutamic acid at position 6 of beta-globin of HbA. It has an autosomal recessive inheritance. There are many variants like hemoglobin SC disease, hemoglobin S beta thalassemia disease, etc. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Erythrocyte size, distribution, and shape variation can all be clues to clinical disease and pathologic processes. For example, acanthocytes may be a byproduct of eryptosis or organized erythrocyte breakdown and death. This process of tabulated cell destruction has been seen in anemia and excess calcium. Agglutination of red blood cells can be an indication of a hypercoagulable state, and microcytosis or small red blood cells can be associated with different forms of microcytic anemia, such as iron deficiency and thalassemia. [8] Additionally, red blood cell morphology has a significant role in the severity of the disease. For example, in sickle cell disease, the concentration of inherited hemoglobin S determines the degree of erythrocyte sickling.

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral blood smear and urine analysis are essential for distinguishing between intravascular and extravascular hemolysis in normocytic anemia. The presence of schistocytes and hemoglobin in the urine suggests microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. However, despite suspected hemolysis, the absence of schistocytes in the peripheral blood smear and the absence of hemoglobin in the urine warrant further investigation. Detection of spherocytes in the peripheral blood smear prompts a direct Coombs test to differentiate autoimmune hemolytic anemia from splenic sequestration in hereditary spherocytosis, aiding diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intracorpuscular causes refer to abnormalities in the red blood cell itself. A red blood cell can be internally damaged when the solubility of hemoglobin is altered (hemoglobinopathy), the structure of the membrane or cytoskeleton is changed (membranopathy), or its metabolic abilities (enzymopathy) is decreased. Examples of hemoglobinopathies include sickle cell disease (SCD) and thalassemias. SCD is caused by a beta-globin gene mutation leading to polymerization of hemoglobin-S, sticking, and, therefore, hemolysis. Thalassemia is the most common cause of hereditary hemolytic anemia and is caused by partial or complete lack of synthesis of one of the major alpha or beta globin chains of hemoglobin A. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Poikilocytosis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Spherocytes; Spherocytes are small round cells that lack the flat, light-colored center of regular erythrocytes. The hereditary form is autosomal dominant in roughly 75% of cases, while the remaining 25% are autosomal recessive or de novo. [36] The hereditary forms have gene mutations SPTA1, SPTB, ANK1, SLC4A1, and EPB42 that code for proteins spectrin, ankyrin, band 3, and erythrocyte protein band 4.2. The resultant dysmorphisms arise from their decreased membrane surface area with decreased membrane flexibility and increased fragility. The cells become rounded and lose their central pallor. They become more easily captured and prematurely destroyed by the spleen. Sometimes the hemolysis may be significant in that the bone marrow initiates a massive (reactive) production of young red blood cells, called reticulocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Elliptocytosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary elliptocytosis, or hereditary ovalocytosis, is an inherited heterogeneous red blood cell (RBC) disorder characterized by elongated, oval, or elliptical-shaped RBCs on the peripheral blood smear. Genetic alterations in α-spectrin, β-spectrin, protein 4.1, band 3, and, rarely, glycophorin C result in the loss of the normal elastic recoil property in RBCs within the peripheral circulation, resulting in their distinctive elliptical shape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: that warrant careful ophthalmologic monitoring in patients with thalassemic disorders. Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) is clinically associated with thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Both disorders are hemoglobinopathies that can coexist in the same patient, and the presence of thalassemia may influence the ocular manifestations observed in sickle cell disease. Recognizing this association underscores the importance of comprehensive ophthalmic assessment and coordinated care for individuals affected by either or both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) includes as a specific manifestation the condition of fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية), indicating that fetal erythroblastosis is a particular subtype or child entity within the broader category of neonatal disorders. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. This condition is clinically associated with قَرحَة (ulcer), reflecting the propensity for vaso‑occlusive events and tissue ischemia that can lead to ulcer formation. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (blood oxygen deficiency or hypoxemia), which results from impaired red‑cell flexibility and consequent reduced oxygen delivery. Recognizing that sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) can present with ulcers and low blood‑oxygen levels is important for comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Prednison may be used to treat فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia). By suppressing the immune response, prednison reduces the production of auto‑antibodies that target red blood cells, thereby decreasing hemolysis and improving anemia. This therapeutic option is important for managing patients whose red blood cell destruction is driven by an autoimmune process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: stage. Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: patients with thalassemia may develop retinal complications characteristic of sickle‑cell disease. All of the aforementioned anemia subtypes—فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية—as well as التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) are listed as members of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), the standardized medical query for immune‑mediated or autoimmune disorders. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is defined as a non‑immune hemolytic anemia, it is still grouped within the immune‑disorder category for classification and reporting purposes, reflecting a pragmatic rather than a pathophysiological contradiction. Together, these relationships illustrate how various deficiency and hemolytic anemias are organized under a common classification, how specific anemias relate to ocular complications, and how many of these
